exemplar.title: Investigating the framing effect in university admission data

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the framing effect in university admission data

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim and relevance are clearly stated and explicitly linked to framingâeffect theory
Theory/model is accurately described (Kahneman & Tversky) and connected to the current manipulation
Variables are correctly operationalized and the directional hypothesis (oneâtailed) is justified
weaknesses: Explanation of the aim is concise and could be enriched with deeper discussion of decision heuristics
Hypothesis rationale could mention why a oneâtailed test is preferred over twoâtailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the topâband descriptors: the aim is stated and its relevance fully explained, the theory is described and explicitly linked to the study, and the IV and DV are operationalized in a clear directional hypothesis. Minor suggestions for deeper elaboration do not detract from overall excellence, warranting the full 6 marks.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is not only named but its rationale (avoiding awareness of other condition) is explained
Stratified sampling of seniors is described and choice justified (control of ability and selfâesteem)
Controlled variables and materials are listed with clear explanations, and ethical safeguards are well detailed
weaknesses: Procedure for random allocation is not specified (e.g., random number generator)
Method for screening participantsâ selfâesteem levels is not explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand is in the 3â4 band and merits the top mark: design, sampling, and materials/controls are all explained rather than merely described. The minor omissions around randomisation and screening donât outweigh the overall solid explanation, so the maximum 4 marks are awarded.

strengths: Descriptive (means, SDs) and inferential (independentâsamples t-test) statistics are appropriately and accurately applied
Statistical interpretation links pâ0.011 to hypothesis rejection and ties back to framingâeffect theory
Reporting is consistent and internally coherent, demonstrating strong quantitative reasoning
weaknesses: Graphical presentation lacks error bars and direct sideâbyâside comparison
Assumption checks (e.g., variance equality) are not reported
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and interpretation are of an exceptionally high standard with only minor graphical and assumptionâtesting omissions. The correct choice and application of inferential tests, plus theoryâlinked interpretation, justify the full 6 marks despite small presentation weaknesses.

strengths: Findings are discussed in reference to the framingâeffect theory
Strengths and limitations are both stated, described, and clearly relevant to the investigation
A modification is explicitly linked to a limitation and fully justified
weaknesses: Discussion is relatively brief and lacks deeper engagement with prior literature or alternative explanations
Only one modification is proposed, limiting the breadth of evaluative suggestions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets the 3â4 band: findings discussion ties to theory, strengths/limitations are explained, and a justified modification is given. However, the discussionâs depth and the single modification limit the mark to 4 of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Graphs are present and use correct bar-chart conventions; however, separate plots per condition do not facilitate direct comparison, axis titles lack units, and no error bars are shown. Thus the presentation addresses the hypothesis but contains notable shortcomings, fitting a 3 in the middle band.

The student states the significant p-value, explicitly links this to rejection of the null and confirmation of the framing-effect hypothesis, and ties the conclusion back to theory. Interpretation is accurate and fully linked, warranting a 5 in the highest band.

Both IV (wording: âaccepts 1 in 5â vs ârejects 4 in 5â) and DV (self-rated chance on a 1â10 scale) are explicitly stated and operationalised. A directional (one-tailed) research hypothesis and an appropriate null are provided, earning a strong 5 in the top band.

Sampling is described (stratified selection of 37 seniors) and the choice is justified by aiming to control academic ability and self-esteem. Explanation of why these characteristics matter places it firmly in the 3â4 band, nearer the upper mark.

The framing effect is accurately described with reference to Kahneman & Tversky and the classic disease paradigm. The student then explains how the present admission-question manipulation embodies that theory. Description and connection are both sound though not exceptionally detailed, warranting a 5 in the highest band.

Findings are discussed in relation to the framing-effect theory, but the discussion is brief and largely reiterates the statistical conclusion without deeper theoretical integration or comparison with past research. This matches the descriptor for the 3â4 band at the lower mark.

The student clearly states the investigationâs aim (to examine whether positive vs negative framing of a 20 % university-admission chance influences self-rated acceptance likelihood) and then explicitly explains why this aim is relevant to the framing-effect theory. The link between aim and cognitive bias is explicit and convincing, but the explanation is brief rather than richly developed, so a solid 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Two clear strengths and two limitations are identified and each is explained in terms of methodological impact, meeting the criteria for explanation and relevance. Coverage is adequate but not exhaustive, so a 4 is appropriate within this band.

Controlled variables are tabulated with clear explanations, and materials are listed with purpose statements. The rationale for each control/material shows understanding, meriting the explained (3â4) band; comprehensiveness supports a 4.

One modification (use of fictional scenario) is explicitly linked to a stated limitation (prior knowledge of real university) with justification. However, only a single modification is offered and justification is fairly concise, positioning it at the lower end of the 3â4 band.

The independent-measures design is not merely named; its rationale (prevent awareness of other condition) is articulated, fulfilling the âexplainedâ requirement. Minor omissions (no discussion of random allocation procedure) keep it at the lower end of the top band.

Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied appropriately: means, SDs, and an independent-samples t-test with correct justification of one-tailed Î± = 0.05. Calculations appear internally consistent. Minor reporting gaps (no test of variance equality) prevent full marks but still justify a 5 in the top band.

annotations.comment: The student offers a precise definition of the framing effect and cites Plous (1993), demonstrating solid grounding in cognitive bias theory. Consider briefly linking this definition back to decision heuristics for greater depth.
The classic Kahneman & Tversky epidemic experiment is described accurately and in sufficient detail, reinforcing the theoretical basis for the studyâs manipulation.
The aim statement begins well but trails off. Complete this sentence to explicitly state the full objective and how participantsâ selfârated scores will test the framing theory.
Operationalization of the dependent variable is clear: participants rate their acceptance likelihood on a 1â10 scale directly tied to the manipulation.
The directional hypothesis is justified, but consider explaining why a oneâtailed test is preferred over a twoâtailed approach in this context.
The independent-measures design rationale is well articulated, emphasizing avoidance of demand characteristics.
It would strengthen the design description to specify how random assignment was implemented (e.g., random number generator, sealed envelopes).
The controlled-variables table is comprehensive, addressing key confounds such as prior knowledge, ability, self-esteem, and university relation.
Clarify how participantsâ self-esteem levels were assessed or screened to ensure the control was effectively applied.
The materials table lists each document with clear usage descriptions, reflecting careful procedural planning.
Ethical safeguards are well detailed, including confidentiality, data deletion, and participant rights, enhancing the studyâs integrity.
Descriptive statistics (means, medians, SDs) are applied correctly and support initial data exploration.
The inclusion of exhaustive descriptive metrics can overwhelm the reader. Focus on those most relevant (e.g., mean, SD, range) to maintain clarity.
Was the assumption of normality tested (e.g., via ShapiroâWilk) before conducting the t-test? Reporting this would strengthen inference validity.
Separate graphs for each group impede direct comparison and omit error bars, limiting insight into variability.
Consider a combined bar chart or violin plot with error bars to facilitate direct comparison of means and variability between conditions.
The independent-samples t-test is appropriate for the design and is reported with necessary test statistics.
Include a homogeneity of variances check (e.g., Leveneâs test) to confirm that t-test assumptions are met.
The discussion reiterates results but lacks deeper engagement with prior literature or alternative explanations.
The strength of the independent-measures design is well explained, emphasizing reduced bias and increased reliability.
Separate strengths and limitations into distinct sections or paragraphs for clearer evaluation structure and focus.
Linking the fictional-scenario modification directly to the limitation demonstrates strong evaluative reasoning and relevance.
Consider proposing additional modifications (e.g., alternative sampling methods, measurement checks) to address other limitations such as generalizability.
The reference list is concise but could be expanded with recent empirical studies on framing effects to enhance contextual depth.
The interpretation correctly links pâ0.011 to rejecting the null and supports the framingâeffect hypothesis.
